name: Priya Sharma
occupation: Recent M.A. graduate, part-time freelance writer
education: M.A. English Literature (2025), B.A. English and Communications
backstory: Priya finished her master's degree six months ago and is struggling to turn her humanities education into a career. Her family immigrated from India when she was seven, and her parents sacrificed a lot so she could get an education — which adds pressure to find 'a real job' quickly. She's been freelance writing to pay bills, but the income is inconsistent and the work feels disconnected from what she studied. She's interested in publishing, content strategy, communications, or even EdTech, but doesn't know how to position herself for any of these. Her sister Anita, an engineer, keeps sending her job links that don't match her skills at all. Priya journals daily and is a thoughtful, careful person — but she's paralyzed by too many options and not enough clarity on what she's qualified for.
career_goals: Find a full-time role that uses her writing, research, and communication skills
Understand which industries and roles value a humanities background
Build confidence that her education and freelance work are marketable
challenges: Doesn't know how to translate an M.A. in English into job qualifications
Freelance work feels informal — unsure how to present it on a resume
Overwhelmed by the range of possible career paths
Family pressure to find stable employment quickly
Many entry-level roles in her fields of interest pay poorly or require unpaid internships
Nervous about salary negotiation — doesn't want to seem ungrateful or pushy
existing_strengths: Excellent writer and editor — published thesis and several freelance pieces
Strong research skills honed through graduate work
Consistent journaling habit — processes decisions well in writing
Empathetic communicator — good at understanding audience needs
Active in a neighborhood writing group — has a small professional network she doesn't realize is valuable
current_situation: Priya found three postings: a Content Strategist role at an EdTech startup, a Communications Coordinator at a nonprofit, and an Editorial Assistant position at a university press. She's excited but anxious — one requires '2+ years of content strategy experience,' another mentions 'experience with CMS platforms,' and the third pays $38k which feels low for Chicago. She wants help figuring out which ones to apply to, how to present her background, and whether the low-paying role is worth it.
what_success_looks_like: Priya has a clear understanding of which roles fit her skills, a resume that frames her academic and freelance work as professional experience, knows what red flags to watch for in job postings (especially around pay), and feels confident enough to apply without spiraling into self-doubt.
